content:
# Exodus Chapter 13 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~40r–40v (PDF pp. 75–76); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 53–54 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_013_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_013_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_013_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_013_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## Consecration of the Firstborn (vv. 1–2)

**1** And YHWH spoke to Mosheh, saying:

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (vayedabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor). The verb דבר (dibber, "to speak") resumes the divine speech — now addressed to Mosheh alone (contrast 12:1, where both Mosheh and Aharon were addressed). This chapter opens a new legislative unit: the consecration of the firstborn and the observance of matstsot in the land. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγων.

**2** "Consecrate [qaddesh] to Me every bekhor [firstborn], the opener of every rechem [womb] among the sons of Yisra'el, among adam [man] and among behemah [beast] — it [is] Mine."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads קַדֶּשׁ־לִי כָל־בְּכוֹר פֶּטֶר כָּל־רֶחֶם בִּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל בָּאָדָם וּבַבְּהֵמָה לִי הוּא (qaddesh-li khol-bekhor peter kol-rekhem bi-vnei Yisra'el ba-adam u-va-behemah li hu, "consecrate to Me every firstborn, opener of every womb among the sons of Yisra'el, among man and among beast — to Me it [is]"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the claim of the firstborn:** The imperative קַדֶּשׁ (qaddesh) = piel of קדשׁ (qadash, "to be holy") — causative: "make holy, set apart, consecrate." The root means separation for divine purpose. The phrase פֶּטֶר רֶחֶם (peter rekhem) = "opener of the womb" — from פטר (patar, "to open, release, separate") + רֶחֶם (rekhem, "womb"). The peter rekhem is the firstborn that "breaks open" the mother's womb — the first issue. The emphatic לִי הוּא (li hu, "to Me it [is]") — the firstborn belongs to YHWH. The theological logic: because YHWH struck every firstborn of Mitsrayim and spared every firstborn of Yisra'el, the spared firstborn now belongs to YHWH as His own. Life spared is life claimed. The scope is comprehensive: בָּאָדָם וּבַבְּהֵמָה (ba-adam u-va-behemah, "among man and among beast") — both human and animal firstborn. LXX: ἁγίασόν μοι πᾶν πρωτότοκον πρωτογενὲς διανοῖγον πᾶσαν μήτραν ἐν τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ ἀπὸ ἀνθρώπου ἕως κτήνους· ἐμοί ἐστιν ("sanctify to me every firstborn, first-begotten, opening every womb among the sons of Israel, from man to beast — it is mine") — ἁγιάζω ("sanctify") for qaddesh; διανοίγω μήτραν ("opening a womb") for peter rekhem.

---

## The Observance of Matstsot and the Teaching of Sons (vv. 3–10)

**3** And Mosheh said to the people: "Remember [zakhor] this day, in which you went out from Mitsrayim, from a house of slaves [mi-beit avadim], for by strength of hand [be-khozeq yad] YHWH brought you out from this [place]; and chamets [leaven] shall not be eaten.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָעָם זָכוֹר אֶת־הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר יְצָאתֶם מִמִּצְרַיִם מִבֵּית עֲבָדִים כִּי בְּחֹזֶק יָד הוֹצִיא יְהוָה אֶתְכֶם מִזֶּה וְלֹא יֵאָכֵל חָמֵץ (vayomer Mosheh el-ha-am zakhor et-ha-yom ha-zeh asher yetsatem mi-Mitsrayim mi-beit avadim ki be-khozeq yad hotsi YHWH etkhem mi-zeh ve-lo ye'akhel chamets). **Key terms:** זָכוֹר (zakhor) = "remember!" — the infinitive absolute used as imperative, from זכר (zakhar, "to remember"). Active, engaged remembering — not mere mental recollection but liturgical re-enactment. The phrase בֵּית עֲבָדִים (beit avadim) = "house of slaves" — Mitsrayim is defined by its essential character: a house of bondage. The phrase בְּחֹזֶק יָד (be-khozeq yad) = "by strength of hand" — from חזק (khazaq, "to be strong") + יָד (yad, "hand"). YHWH's hand is the instrument of deliverance — the same root used for the hardening of Par'oh's heart (חזק) now describes YHWH's liberating power. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ Μωυσῆς πρὸς τὸν λαόν· μνημονεύετε τὴν ἡμέραν ταύτην ἐν ᾗ ἐξήλθατε ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου ἐξ οἴκου δουλείας· ἐν γὰρ χειρὶ κραταιᾷ ἐξήγαγεν ὑμᾶς κύριος ἐντεῦθεν· καὶ οὐ βρωθήσεται ζύμη ("remember this day in which you went out from the land of Egypt, from a house of slavery; for with a mighty hand the Lord brought you out from here; and leaven shall not be eaten") — χεὶρ κραταιά ("mighty hand") for khozeq yad.

**4** Today you [are] going out, in the month of the Aviv [ha-Aviv].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads הַיּוֹם אַתֶּם יֹצְאִים בְּחֹדֶשׁ הָאָבִיב (ha-yom attem yots'im be-khodesh ha-Aviv, "today you [are] going out in the month of the Aviv"). **Key term:** אָבִיב (Aviv) = "fresh, young ears [of grain]" — the month when barley ripens, later called Nisan. The name encodes the agricultural season: spring, new growth, the awakening of the land after winter. This is the only place in the chapter where the month is named — and it is named not by number but by agricultural character. The definite article הָאָבִיב (ha-Aviv, "the Aviv") makes it a proper noun. LXX: ἐν γὰρ τῇ σήμερον ὑμεῖς ἐκπορεύεσθε ἐν μηνὶ τῶν νέων ("for on this day you are going out in the month of the new [grain]") — τὰ νέα ("new things, new grain") for Aviv.

**5** And it shall be, when YHWH brings you [yevi'akha] to the land of the Kena'ani and the Chitti and the Emori and the Chivvi and the Yevusi, which He swore to your fathers to give to you — a land flowing [with] chalav [milk] and devash [honey] — then you shall serve [ve-avadta] this avodah [service] in this month.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה כִי־יְבִיאֲךָ יְהוָה אֶל־אֶרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַחִתִּי וְהָאֱמֹרִי וְהַחִוִּי וְהַיְבוּסִי אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע לַאֲבֹתֶיךָ לָתֶת לָךְ אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ וְעָבַדְתָּ אֶת־הָעֲבֹדָה הַזֹּאת בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה (ve-hayah khi-yevi'akha YHWH el-erets ha-Kena'ani ve-ha-Chitti ve-ha-Emori ve-ha-Chivvi ve-ha-Yevusi asher nishba la-avoteicha latet lakh erets zavat chalav u-devash ve-avadta et-ha-avodah ha-zot ba-khodesh ha-zeh). **Key phrase:** אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ (erets zavat chalav u-devash) = "a land flowing [with] milk and honey" — the verb זָבַת (zavat) from זוב (zuv, "to flow, gush"). The land overflows with abundance. חָלָב (chalav) = "milk" — pastoral abundance; דְּבָשׁ (devash) = "honey" — whether bee-honey or date-syrup, agricultural sweetness. Five nations are listed (contrast the fuller lists of six or seven elsewhere) — the Kena'ani, Chitti, Emori, Chivvi, and Yevusi represent the peoples inhabiting the promised land. The word עֲבֹדָה (avodah) = "service, worship" — the same word that described Egyptian slavery now describes sacred observance, as in 12:25–26. LXX: καὶ ἔσται ἡνίκα ἐὰν εἰσαγάγῃ σε κύριος ὁ θεός σου εἰς τὴν γῆν τῶν Χαναναίων καὶ Χετταίων καὶ Αμορραίων καὶ Ευαίων καὶ Ιεβουσαίων, ἣν ὤμοσεν τοῖς πατράσιν σου δοῦναί σοι, γῆν ῥέουσαν γάλα καὶ μέλι — ῥέω ("to flow") for zavat.

**6** Seven days you shall eat matstsot [unleavened bread], and on the seventh day [there shall be] a chag [feast] to YHWH.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads שִׁבְעַת יָמִים תֹּאכַל מַצֹּת וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי חַג לַיהוָה (shiv'at yamim tokhal matstsot u-va-yom ha-shevi'i khag la-YHWH, "seven days you shall eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day [there shall be] a feast to YHWH"). The seven-day framework recapitulates 12:15–20 but in briefer form. The seventh day is marked as a חַג (khag) = "feast, festival" — the culmination of the week of matstsot. LXX: ἓξ ἡμέρας ἔδεσθε ἄζυμα, τῇ δὲ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἑβδόμῃ ἑορτὴ κυρίῳ — **textual note:** LXX reads ἕξ ("six") rather than MT's שִׁבְעַת ("seven") for the days of eating matstsot. This is a notable variant; the LXX may reckon the seventh day as feast-only rather than a day of eating matstsot.

**7** Matstsot [unleavened bread] shall be eaten the seven days; and chamets [leaven] shall not be seen for you, and se'or [leavening-agent] shall not be seen for you in all your territory [gevulekha].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads מַצּוֹת יֵאָכֵל אֵת שִׁבְעַת הַיָּמִים וְלֹא־יֵרָאֶה לְךָ חָמֵץ וְלֹא־יֵרָאֶה לְךָ שְׂאֹר בְּכָל־גְּבֻלֶךָ (matstsot ye'akhel et shiv'at ha-yamim ve-lo-yera'eh lekha chamets ve-lo-yera'eh lekha se'or be-khol-gevulekha, "unleavened bread shall be eaten the seven days; and leaven shall not be seen for you, and leavening-agent shall not be seen for you in all your territory"). **Key distinction:** Two separate terms for the forbidden substance: חָמֵץ (chamets) = "leavened bread" (the product) and שְׂאֹר (se'or) = "leaven, sourdough starter" (the fermenting agent itself), as in 12:15. The verb יֵרָאֶה (yera'eh) = niphal of ראה (ra'ah, "to see") — "shall not be seen." Not merely "you shall not eat" but "it shall not even be visible" — a stricter standard. The word גְּבוּל (gevul) = "border, territory" — the prohibition extends to the entire territorial boundary. LXX: ἄζυμα ἔδεσθε τὰς ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας· οὐκ ὀφθήσεταί σοι ζυμωτόν, οὐδὲ ἔσται σοι ζύμη ἐν πᾶσιν τοῖς ὁρίοις σου.

**8** And you shall tell [ve-higgadta] your son on that day, saying: 'On account of this [ba-avur zeh] YHWH did [it] for me when I went out from Mitsrayim.'

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהִגַּדְתָּ לְבִנְךָ בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא לֵאמֹר בַּעֲבוּר זֶה עָשָׂה יְהוָה לִי בְּצֵאתִי מִמִּצְרָיִם (ve-higgadta le-vinkha ba-yom ha-hu lemor ba-avur zeh asah YHWH li be-tseiti mi-Mitsrayim, "and you shall tell your son on that day, saying: on account of this YHWH did for me when I went out from Mitsrayim"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the pedagogy of memory:** The verb הִגַּדְתָּ (higgadta) = hiphil of נגד (nagad, "to declare, tell, announce") — this is the root of הַגָּדָה (Haggadah), the Passover telling. The parent is commanded to tell, not merely to observe. The phrase בַּעֲבוּר זֶה (ba-avur zeh, "on account of this") is ambiguous by design: "this" could point forward (the observance: "it is because of this [observance] that YHWH acted for me") or backward (the exodus event: "because of this [event] we observe"). The phrase לִי (li, "for me") — first person singular: each generation speaks as if they themselves went out. Not "for our ancestors" but "for me." This is the principle of personal identification with the exodus that structures the Passover seder. LXX: καὶ ἀναγγελεῖς τῷ υἱῷ σου ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ λέγων· διὰ τοῦτο ἐποίησεν κύριος ὁ θεός μοι ὡς ἐξεπορευόμην ἐξ Αἰγύπτου ("and you shall declare to your son on that day, saying: because of this the Lord God did for me when I was going out from Egypt").

**9** And it shall be for you for a sign [le-ot] upon your hand and for a remembrance [le-zikkaron] between your eyes, so that the torah [instruction] of YHWH shall be in your mouth; for by a strong hand [be-yad chazaqah] YHWH brought you out from Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה לְךָ לְאוֹת עַל־יָדְךָ וּלְזִכָּרוֹן בֵּין עֵינֶיךָ לְמַעַן תִּהְיֶה תּוֹרַת יְהוָה בְּפִיךָ כִּי בְּיָד חֲזָקָה הוֹצִאֲךָ יְהוָה מִמִּצְרָיִם (ve-hayah lekha le-ot al-yadkha u-le-zikkaron bein einekha lema'an tihyeh torat YHWH be-fikha ki be-yad khazaqah hotsi'akha YHWH mi-Mitsrayim, "and it shall be for you for a sign upon your hand and for a remembrance between your eyes, so that the instruction of YHWH shall be in your mouth; for by a strong hand YHWH brought you out from Mitsrayim"). **Key terms:** אוֹת (ot) = "sign" — from אות, a visible marker. The phrase עַל־יָדְךָ (al-yadkha, "upon your hand") and בֵּין עֵינֶיךָ (bein einekha, "between your eyes") — the two locations that later rabbinic tradition interprets as the placement of תְּפִלִּין (tefillin, phylacteries). Whether the original meaning is literal (a physical object bound to hand and forehead) or metaphorical (constant awareness, as in Prov 3:3, 6:21, 7:3) is debated. The word זִכָּרוֹן (zikkaron) = "remembrance, memorial" — from זכר (zakhar, "to remember"), as in 12:14. The phrase תּוֹרַת יְהוָה בְּפִיךָ (torat YHWH be-fikha, "the instruction of YHWH in your mouth") — the goal of the sign: the mouth speaks YHWH's teaching. The sign on the body leads to the word in the mouth. LXX: καὶ ἔσται σοι σημεῖον ἐπὶ τῆς χειρός σου καὶ μνημόσυνον πρὸ ὀφθαλμῶν σου, ὅπως ἂν γένηται ὁ νόμος κυρίου ἐν τῷ στόματί σου· ἐν γὰρ χειρὶ κραταιᾷ ἐξήγαγέν σε κύριος ὁ θεὸς ἐξ Αἰγύπτου — σημεῖον ("sign") for ot; μνημόσυνον ("memorial") for zikkaron.

**10** And you shall keep [ve-shamarta] this chuqqah [statute] at its appointed-time [le-mo'adah], from days to days [mi-yamim yamimah]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְשָׁמַרְתָּ אֶת־הַחֻקָּה הַזֹּאת לְמוֹעֲדָהּ מִיָּמִים יָמִימָה (ve-shamarta et-ha-khuqqah ha-zot le-mo'adah mi-yamim yamimah, "and you shall keep this statute at its appointed-time, from days to days"). **Key terms:** חֻקָּה (khuqqah) = "statute, ordinance" — from חקק (khaqaq, "to engrave, inscribe, decree"). מוֹעֵד (mo'ed) = "appointed-time, set feast" — from יעד (ya'ad, "to appoint, designate"). The phrase מִיָּמִים יָמִימָה (mi-yamim yamimah) = "from days to days" = "year by year, annually." The same phrase appears in Judges 11:40, 21:19 and 1 Samuel 1:3. The statute is not one-time but cyclical, returning at its appointed season each year. LXX: καὶ φυλάξεσθε τὸν νόμον τοῦτον κατὰ καιροὺς ὡρῶν ἀφ᾽ ἡμερῶν εἰς ἡμέρας ("and you shall keep this law according to its seasons, from days to days").

---

## The Law of the Firstborn (vv. 11–16)

**11** And it shall be, when YHWH brings you to the land of the Kena'ani, as He swore to you and to your fathers, and gives it to you —

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה כִּי־יְבִאֲךָ יְהוָה אֶל־אֶרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי כַּאֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע לְךָ וְלַאֲבֹתֶיךָ וּנְתָנָהּ לָךְ (ve-hayah ki-yevi'akha YHWH el-erets ha-Kena'ani ka-asher nishba lekha ve-la-avotekha u-netanah lakh, "and it shall be when YHWH brings you to the land of the Kena'ani, as He swore to you and to your fathers, and gives it to you"). This verse echoes v. 5, again introduced by the formula וְהָיָה כִּי (ve-hayah ki, "and it shall be when...") — projecting into the future possession of the land. Here only the Kena'ani is named, representing the totality of the land's inhabitants (as the umbrella term). LXX: καὶ ἔσται ὡς ἂν εἰσαγάγῃ σε κύριος ὁ θεός σου εἰς τὴν γῆν τῶν Χαναναίων ὃν τρόπον ὤμοσεν τοῖς πατράσιν σου, καὶ δώσει σοι αὐτήν.

**12** then you shall cause to pass over [ve-ha'avarta] every opener of a womb [peter-rekhem] to YHWH; and every opener [peter] of offspring [sheger] of a behemah [beast] which shall be yours — the males [ha-zekharim] [shall be] to YHWH.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהַעֲבַרְתָּ כָל־פֶּטֶר־רֶחֶם לַיהוָה וְכָל־פֶּטֶר שֶׁגֶר בְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר יִהְיֶה לְךָ הַזְּכָרִים לַיהוָה (ve-ha'avarta khol-peter-rekhem la-YHWH ve-khol-peter sheger behemah asher yihyeh lekha ha-zekharim la-YHWH, "and you shall cause to pass over every opener of a womb to YHWH, and every opener of offspring of a beast which shall be yours — the males to YHWH"). **Key terms:** וְהַעֲבַרְתָּ (ve-ha'avarta) = hiphil of עבר (avar, "to pass over, transfer") — the same root as the Passover night's "passing through" (12:12), now used for the transfer/dedication of firstborn to YHWH. The verb implies handing over, setting aside, transferring ownership. שֶׁגֶר (sheger) = "offspring, issue" — a rare word, from שגר, referring to the young of domestic animals. The specification הַזְּכָרִים (ha-zekharim, "the males") narrows the requirement to male firstborn. LXX: καὶ ἀφελεῖς πᾶν διανοῖγον μήτραν, τὰ ἀρσενικά, τῷ κυρίῳ· πᾶν διανοῖγον μήτραν ἐκ τῶν βουκολίων ἢ ἐν τοῖς κτήνεσίν σου, ὅσα ἐὰν γένηταί σοι, τὰ ἀρσενικὰ ἁγιάσεις τῷ κυρίῳ ("you shall set apart every opener of the womb, the males, to the Lord") — ἀφαιρέω ("set apart, take away") for ha'avarta.

**13** And every opener [peter] of a chamor [donkey] you shall redeem [tifdeh] with a seh [lamb/kid]; and if you do not redeem [it], then you shall break its neck [va-arafto]; and every bekhor [firstborn] of adam [man] among your sons you shall redeem [tifdeh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכָל־פֶּטֶר חֲמֹר תִּפְדֶּה בְשֶׂה וְאִם־לֹא תִפְדֶּה וַעֲרַפְתּוֹ וְכֹל בְּכוֹר אָדָם בְּבָנֶיךָ תִּפְדֶּה (ve-khol-peter chamor tifdeh ve-seh ve-im-lo tifdeh va-arafto ve-khol bekhor adam be-vanekha tifdeh, "and every opener of a donkey you shall redeem with a lamb/kid, and if you do not redeem, then you shall break its neck; and every firstborn of man among your sons you shall redeem"). **Key terms:** תִּפְדֶּה (tifdeh) = "you shall redeem" — from פדה (padah, "to redeem, ransom"). Redemption is the payment of a substitute to release a firstborn from the obligation of being given to YHWH. חֲמוֹר (chamor) = "donkey" — an unclean animal that cannot be sacrificed. Since it cannot be offered, it must be redeemed (replaced with a seh) or destroyed. The verb וַעֲרַפְתּוֹ (va-arafto) from ערף (araf, "to break the neck") — the only alternative to redemption is destruction: an unredeemed firstborn donkey has its neck broken. The human firstborn must be redeemed — no option of sacrifice exists for human beings. This is the theological foundation of the pidyon ha-ben ("redemption of the [firstborn] son") ceremony. LXX: πᾶν διανοῖγον μήτραν ὄνου ἀλλάξεις προβάτῳ· ἐὰν δὲ μὴ ἀλλάξῃς, λυτρώσῃ αὐτό· πᾶν πρωτότοκον ἀνθρώπου τῶν υἱῶν σου λυτρώσῃ ("every opener of a donkey's womb you shall exchange for a sheep; if you do not exchange, you shall ransom it; every firstborn of man among your sons you shall ransom") — **textual note:** LXX reads λυτρώσῃ ("ransom") for both the donkey alternative and the human firstborn, where MT uses ערף ("break the neck") for the donkey.

**14** And it shall be, when your son asks you tomorrow [makhar], saying: 'What [is] this [mah-zot]?' — then you shall say to him: 'By strength of hand [be-khozeq yad] YHWH brought us out from Mitsrayim, from a house of slaves [mi-beit avadim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה כִּי־יִשְׁאָלְךָ בִנְךָ מָחָר לֵאמֹר מַה־זֹּאת וְאָמַרְתָּ אֵלָיו בְּחֹזֶק יָד הוֹצִיאָנוּ יְהוָה מִמִּצְרַיִם מִבֵּית עֲבָדִים (ve-hayah ki-yish'alkha vinkha makhar lemor mah-zot ve-amarta elav be-khozeq yad hotsi'anu YHWH mi-Mitsrayim mi-beit avadim, "and it shall be when your son asks you tomorrow, saying: 'What is this?' — then you shall say to him: 'By strength of hand YHWH brought us out from Mitsrayim, from a house of slaves'"). **Key phrase:** The child's question מַה־זֹּאת (mah-zot, "what is this?") is simpler than the question in 12:26 (מָה הָעֲבֹדָה הַזֹּאת לָכֶם, "what is this service to you?"). In rabbinic tradition, this becomes the question of the "simple son" (tam) in the four-sons typology of the Haggadah, as opposed to the "wicked son" of 12:26. The word מָחָר (makhar) = "tomorrow" — i.e., in the future, in a coming generation. The answer points to the exodus event: YHWH's strong hand. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἐρωτήσῃ σε ὁ υἱός σου μετὰ ταῦτα λέγων· τί τοῦτο; καὶ ἐρεῖς αὐτῷ ὅτι ἐν χειρὶ κραταιᾷ ἐξήγαγεν ἡμᾶς κύριος ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου ἐξ οἴκου δουλείας — note LXX reads μετὰ ταῦτα ("after these things") for makhar.

**15** And it was, when Par'oh made hard [hiqshah] to send us out, that YHWH killed every bekhor [firstborn] in the land of Mitsrayim, from the bekhor of adam [man] unto the bekhor of behemah [beast]; therefore I [am] sacrificing [zoveach] to YHWH every opener of a womb [peter rekhem] — the males — and every bekhor of my sons I redeem [efdeh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי כִּי־הִקְשָׁה פַרְעֹה לְשַׁלְּחֵנוּ וַיַּהֲרֹג יְהוָה כָּל־בְּכוֹר בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מִבְּכֹר אָדָם וְעַד־בְּכוֹר בְּהֵמָה עַל־כֵּן אֲנִי זֹבֵחַ לַיהוָה כָּל־פֶּטֶר רֶחֶם הַזְּכָרִים וְכָל־בְּכוֹר בָּנַי אֶפְדֶּה (vayehi ki-hiqshah Par'oh le-shallkhenu vayaharog YHWH kol-bekhor be-erets Mitsrayim mi-bekhor adam ve-ad-bekhor behemah al-ken ani zoveakh la-YHWH kol-peter rekhem ha-zekharim ve-khol-bekhor banai efdeh, "and it was, when Par'oh made hard to send us out, that YHWH killed every firstborn in the land of Mitsrayim, from the firstborn of man unto the firstborn of beast; therefore I am sacrificing to YHWH every opener of a womb — the males — and every firstborn of my sons I redeem"). **Key terms:** הִקְשָׁה (hiqshah) = hiphil of קשׁה (qashah, "to be hard, difficult") — Par'oh "made hard" his refusal, another variation on the hardening vocabulary (alongside חזק and כבד). The verb זֹבֵחַ (zoveakh) = "sacrificing" — from זבח (zavakh, "to slaughter, sacrifice") — the male firstborn of animals are sacrificed; the human firstborn are redeemed (אֶפְדֶּה, efdeh). The theological logic is laid out explicitly: because YHWH killed Egypt's firstborn (judgment), Israel's firstborn belong to YHWH (consecration), and are either sacrificed (animals) or redeemed (humans). LXX: ἡνίκα δὲ ἐσκλήρυνεν Φαραω ἐξαποστεῖλαι ἡμᾶς, ἀπέκτεινεν πᾶν πρωτότοκον ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ... διὰ τοῦτο ἐγὼ θύω τῷ κυρίῳ πᾶν διανοῖγον μήτραν, τὰ ἀρσενικά, καὶ πᾶν πρωτότοκον τῶν υἱῶν μου λυτρώσομαι — σκληρύνω ("harden") for hiqshah.

**16** And it shall be for a sign [le-ot] upon your hand and for totafot [frontlets] between your eyes; for by strength of hand [be-khozeq yad] YHWH brought us out from Mitsrayim.'"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה לְאוֹת עַל־יָדְכָה וּלְטוֹטָפֹת בֵּין עֵינֶיךָ כִּי בְּחֹזֶק יָד הוֹצִיאָנוּ יְהוָה מִמִּצְרָיִם (ve-hayah le-ot al-yadkhah u-le-totafot bein einekha ki be-khozeq yad hotsi'anu YHWH mi-Mitsrayim, "and it shall be for a sign upon your hand and for frontlets between your eyes; for by strength of hand YHWH brought us out from Mitsrayim"). **Key term:** טוֹטָפֹת (totafot) = "frontlets, bands" — a word of uncertain etymology, appearing only in Exodus 13:16, Deuteronomy 6:8, and 11:18. Rabbinic tradition identifies totafot with tefillin (phylacteries) — small boxes containing Torah passages bound to the forehead. The etymology may be non-Semitic; some derive it from an old word meaning "ornament" or "band." In v. 9 the parallel term was זִכָּרוֹן (zikkaron, "remembrance"); here it is טוֹטָפֹת (totafot, "frontlets") — a concrete, physical term. The phrase בְּחֹזֶק יָד (be-khozeq yad, "by strength of hand") is the refrain of this section (vv. 3, 9, 14, 16) — the strong hand of YHWH is the theological anchor of all these observances. LXX: καὶ ἔσται εἰς σημεῖον ἐπὶ τῆς χειρός σου καὶ ἀσάλευτον πρὸ ὀφθαλμῶν σου· ἐν γὰρ χειρὶ κραταιᾷ ἐξήγαγέν σε κύριος ἐξ Αἰγύπτου ("and it shall be for a sign upon your hand and immovable before your eyes") — ἀσάλευτον ("immovable, unshakeable") for totafot — a remarkable interpretive rendering suggesting permanence and stability.

---

## The Route Through the Wilderness (vv. 17–18)

**17** And it was, when Par'oh sent out the people, that Elohim did not lead them [by] the way of the land of the Pelishtim, although it [was] near [qarov]; for Elohim said: "Lest the people change-their-minds [yinnakhem] when they see milchamah [war], and they return [ve-shavu] to Mitsrayim."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי בְּשַׁלַּח פַּרְעֹה אֶת־הָעָם וְלֹא־נָחָם אֱלֹהִים דֶּרֶךְ אֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים כִּי קָרוֹב הוּא כִּי אָמַר אֱלֹהִים פֶּן־יִנָּחֵם הָעָם בִּרְאֹתָם מִלְחָמָה וְשָׁבוּ מִצְרָיְמָה (vayehi be-shallakh Par'oh et-ha-am ve-lo-nakham Elohim derekh erets Pelishtim ki qarov hu ki amar Elohim pen-yinnakhem ha-am bir'otam milkhamah ve-shavu Mitsraymah). **Key terms:** The verb נָחָם (nakham) appears twice with different meanings: (1) וְלֹא־נָחָם (ve-lo-nakham) = "He did not lead them" — from נחה (nakhah, "to lead, guide"); (2) פֶּן־יִנָּחֵם (pen-yinnakhem) = "lest they change-their-minds / regret / repent" — from נחם (nakham, "to be sorry, comfort, change one's mind"). The wordplay is deliberate: God did not "lead" (nakhah) by the near way, lest the people "repent/regret" (nakham). The phrase דֶּרֶךְ אֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים (derekh erets Pelishtim, "way of the land of the Pelishtim") = the coastal route along the Mediterranean, the most direct path to Canaan — later known as the Via Maris. It was heavily garrisoned by Egyptian military outposts. The theological reasoning: God knows the fragility of newly freed slaves and diverts them from military confrontation that would break their will. LXX: ὡς δὲ ἐξαπέστειλεν Φαραω τὸν λαόν, οὐχ ὡδήγησεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεὸς ὁδὸν γῆς Φυλιστιιμ ὅτι ἐγγὺς ἦν· εἶπεν γὰρ ὁ θεός· μήποτε μεταμελήσῃ τῷ λαῷ ἰδόντι πόλεμον καὶ ἀποστρέψῃ εἰς Αἴγυπτον — μεταμέλομαι ("change one's mind, regret") for yinnakhem.

**18** And Elohim caused the people to turn about [vayassev] [by] the way of the midbar [wilderness] [toward] the Yam-Suf [Sea of Reeds]; and chamushim [in battle-array / armed / in fifths] the sons of Yisra'el went up from the land of Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּסֵּב אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָעָם דֶּרֶךְ הַמִּדְבָּר יַם־סוּף וַחֲמֻשִׁים עָלוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (vayassev Elohim et-ha-am derekh ha-midbar yam-Suf va-chamushim alu venei-Yisra'el me-erets Mitsrayim, "and Elohim caused the people to turn about [by] the way of the wilderness [toward] the Sea of Reeds; and armed/in-battle-array the sons of Yisra'el went up from the land of Mitsrayim"). **Key terms:** וַיַּסֵּב (vayassev) = hiphil of סבב (savav, "to turn, go around") — God caused a detour, a circuitous route through the wilderness rather than the direct coastal road. יַם־סוּף (yam-Suf) = "Sea of Reeds/Rushes" — from סוּף (suf, "reed, rush"), not "Red Sea." The precise location is debated, but the term refers to a body of water characterized by reeds. **Critically debated term:** חֲמֻשִׁים (chamushim) — this word has been interpreted variously: (a) "armed, equipped for battle" — from חמשׁ in the sense of being girded/equipped; (b) "in battle-array, in fifths / in five divisions" — from חָמֵשׁ (khamesh, "five"), suggesting military organization in five columns (vanguard, rear, center, two flanks); (c) "in the fifth generation" — an alternative derivation. The most common interpretation is "armed" or "in martial order" — reinforcing the image of Yisra'el departing as an organized host (cf. 12:41, tsiv'ot). LXX: καὶ ἐκύκλωσεν ὁ θεὸς τὸν λαὸν ὁδὸν τὴν εἰς τὴν ἔρημον εἰς τὴν ἐρυθρὰν θάλασσαν· πέμπτῃ δὲ γενεᾷ ἀνέβησαν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου ("and God led the people around by the way into the wilderness to the Red Sea; and in the fifth generation the sons of Israel went up from the land of Egypt") — **textual note:** LXX reads πέμπτῃ γενεᾷ ("in the fifth generation") for chamushim, reflecting interpretation (c) above; ἐρυθρὰ θάλασσα ("Red Sea") for yam-Suf — the LXX's "Red Sea" rendering has shaped Western translation tradition.

---

## The Bones of Yosef (v. 19)

**19** And Mosheh took the atsmot [bones] of Yosef with him; for he had caused the sons of Yisra'el to swear [hashbea hishbia], saying: "Elohim will surely visit [paqod yifqod] you, and you shall bring up my bones from this [place] with you."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת־עַצְמוֹת יוֹסֵף עִמּוֹ כִּי הַשְׁבֵּעַ הִשְׁבִּיעַ אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֱלֹהִים אֶתְכֶם וְהַעֲלִתֶם אֶת־עַצְמוֹתַי מִזֶּה אִתְּכֶם (vayiqqakh Mosheh et-atsmot Yosef immo ki hashbea hishbia et-benei Yisra'el lemor paqod yifqod Elohim etkhem ve-ha'alitem et-atsmotai mi-zeh ittkhem, "and Mosheh took the bones of Yosef with him; for he had surely caused the sons of Yisra'el to swear, saying: 'Elohim will surely visit you, and you shall bring up my bones from here with you'"). **Key terms:** עַצְמוֹת (atsmot) = "bones" — from עֶצֶם (etsem, "bone"), the same word used in 12:41, 46 for "substance/bone." Yosef's bones are the physical link between the patriarchal promise and the exodus fulfillment. The phrase הַשְׁבֵּעַ הִשְׁבִּיעַ (hashbea hishbia) = infinitive absolute + hiphil of שׁבע (shava, "to swear") = "he surely caused to swear, he solemnly adjured" — the doubled form emphasizes the binding nature of the oath. This oath is recorded in Bereshit 50:25. The phrase פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד (paqod yifqod) = infinitive absolute + qal of פקד (paqad, "to visit, attend to, number, appoint") = "surely visit, certainly attend to" — the verb of divine intervention. YHWH's "visiting" is His active intervention in history. The fulfillment of Yosef's request bridges 400 years — from the death of Yosef in Mitsrayim to the exodus. The bones that Mosheh carries will not be buried until Joshua 24:32, at Shekhem. LXX: καὶ ἔλαβεν Μωυσῆς τὰ ὀστᾶ Ιωσηφ μεθ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ· ὅρκῳ γὰρ ὥρκισεν τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ λέγων· ἐπισκοπῇ ἐπισκέψεται ὑμᾶς κύριος, καὶ συνανοίσετέ μου τὰ ὀστᾶ ἐντεῦθεν μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν ("and Mosheh took the bones of Ioseph with him; for he had adjured the sons of Israel by oath, saying: 'with visitation the Lord will visit you, and you shall bring up my bones from here with you'") — ἐπισκοπή + ἐπισκέπτομαι ("visitation... will visit") for paqod yifqod.

---

## The Journey to Etam — Pillar of Cloud and Fire (vv. 20–22)

**20** And they journeyed from Sukkot and encamped at Etam, at the edge [bi-qtseh] of the midbar [wilderness].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּסְעוּ מִסֻּכֹּת וַיַּחֲנוּ בְאֵתָם בִּקְצֵה הַמִּדְבָּר (vayis'u mi-Sukkot vayakhanu ve-Etam bi-qtseh ha-midbar, "and they journeyed from Sukkot and encamped at Etam, at the edge of the wilderness"). **Key terms:** The verb וַיִּסְעוּ (vayis'u) from נסע (nasa, "to pull up [tent pegs], journey, set out") — the technical term for breaking camp and moving. סֻכֹּת (Sukkot) = "booths" — the first stop after Ra'meses (12:37). אֵתָם (Etam) = the second encampment, at the edge of the wilderness — the threshold between inhabited land and the open desert. The phrase בִּקְצֵה (bi-qtseh) = "at the edge, at the extremity" — from קָצֶה (qatseh, "end, edge"). LXX: ἐξάραντες δὲ οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ἐκ Σοκχωθ ἐστρατοπέδευσαν ἐν Οθομ παρὰ τὴν ἔρημον ("and having set out from Sokcchoth, the sons of Israel encamped at Othom beside the wilderness").

**21** And YHWH [was] going before them by day in a pillar of cloud [be-ammud anan] to lead them [on] the way, and by night in a pillar of fire [be-ammud esh] to give light to them — to go by day and by night.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיהוָה הֹלֵךְ לִפְנֵיהֶם יוֹמָם בְּעַמּוּד עָנָן לַנְחֹתָם הַדֶּרֶךְ וְלַיְלָה בְּעַמּוּד אֵשׁ לְהָאִיר לָהֶם לָלֶכֶת יוֹמָם וָלָיְלָה (va-YHWH holekh lifneihem yomam be-ammud anan lanakhotam ha-derekh ve-lailah be-ammud esh le-ha'ir lahem lalekhet yomam va-lailah, "and YHWH [was] going before them by day in a pillar of cloud to lead them [on] the way, and by night in a pillar of fire to give light to them — to go by day and by night"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the visible presence of YHWH:** This is the first appearance of the עַמּוּד עָנָן (ammud anan, "pillar of cloud") and עַמּוּד אֵשׁ (ammud esh, "pillar of fire") — the visible manifestation of YHWH's guidance. The word עַמּוּד (ammud) = "pillar, column" — from עמד (amad, "to stand"). A standing column, vertical and visible. עָנָן (anan) = "cloud" — the cloud is the medium of divine presence throughout the Pentateuch (cf. 14:19–20, 24; 19:9, 16; 33:9–10; 40:34–38; Num 12:5). The cloud both reveals and conceals: YHWH is present but not fully visible. אֵשׁ (esh) = "fire" — the fire illuminates the night, enabling continuous travel. The dual form (cloud by day, fire by night) means YHWH's guidance never ceases. The verb הֹלֵךְ (holekh) = participle of הלך (halakh, "to walk, go") — YHWH is "walking" before them, a companion on the journey, not a distant deity. The verb לַנְחֹתָם (lanakhotam) from נחה (nakhah, "to lead, guide") — the same root from v. 17 (lo nakham). LXX: ὁ δὲ θεὸς ἡγεῖτο αὐτῶν ἡμέρας μὲν ἐν στύλῳ νεφέλης δεῖξαι αὐτοῖς τὴν ὁδόν, τὴν δὲ νύκτα ἐν στύλῳ πυρὸς φαίνειν αὐτοῖς ("and God was leading them, by day in a pillar of cloud to show them the way, and by night in a pillar of fire to shine for them") — στῦλος ("pillar") for ammud; νεφέλη ("cloud") for anan; πῦρ ("fire") for esh. **Note:** LXX reads ὁ θεός ("God") where MT reads YHWH.

**22** He did not remove [lo-yamish] the pillar of cloud by day and the pillar of fire by night from before the people.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא־יָמִישׁ עַמּוּד הֶעָנָן יוֹמָם וְעַמּוּד הָאֵשׁ לָיְלָה לִפְנֵי הָעָם (lo-yamish ammud he-anan yomam ve-ammud ha-esh lailah lifnei ha-am, "He did not remove the pillar of the cloud by day and the pillar of the fire by night from before the people"). **Key verb:** יָמִישׁ (yamish) = hiphil of מושׁ (mush, "to depart, remove") — "He did not cause to depart." The subject is YHWH (implied from v. 21). The emphatic negative לֹא־יָמִישׁ (lo-yamish) = "He never removed" — expressing continuous, unbroken guidance. The definite articles הֶעָנָן (he-anan, "the cloud") and הָאֵשׁ (ha-esh, "the fire") — now specified with the article, referring back to the pillars just introduced. The verse closes the chapter with an image of permanence: YHWH's guiding presence is constant, unwavering, never withdrawn. This verse forms the transition to chapter 14, where the pillar of cloud will play a decisive role at the Sea. LXX: οὐκ ἐξέλιπεν ὁ στῦλος τῆς νεφέλης ἡμέρας καὶ ὁ στῦλος τοῦ πυρὸς νυκτὸς ἐναντίον παντὸς τοῦ λαοῦ ("the pillar of cloud by day and the pillar of fire by night did not fail before all the people") — ἐκλείπω ("fail, cease, give out") for yamish.

---

## Key Lexical Notes — Exodus 13

- **peter rekhem** (פֶּטֶר רֶחֶם, vv. 2, 12, 13, 15): "opener of the womb" — the firstborn that breaks open the mother's womb. The verb פטר (patar) = "to open, separate, release." The concept is physical: the first issue that opens the birth canal. This firstborn, having been spared when Egypt's firstborn died, now belongs to YHWH. The peter rekhem of a clean animal is sacrificed; the peter rekhem of a donkey (unclean) is redeemed or neck-broken; the peter rekhem of a human is redeemed.

- **qaddesh** (קַדֶּשׁ, v. 2): "consecrate, make holy" — piel imperative of קדשׁ (qadash). The root ק-ד-שׁ denotes separation from the common for sacred purpose. To consecrate the firstborn is to transfer it from human possession to divine ownership. The firstborn is YHWH's because YHWH spared it.

- **chamushim** (חֲמֻשִׁים, v. 18): "armed / in battle-array / in fifths" — one of the most debated words in Exodus. The three main interpretations — armed, organized in five divisions, or in the fifth generation — reflect different derivations. Whatever the precise meaning, the word reinforces the image of Yisra'el departing not as a disordered rabble but as an organized body, consistent with the military language of tsiv'ot ("hosts") in 12:41, 51.

- **totafot** (טוֹטָפֹת, v. 16): "frontlets" — a rare word of uncertain etymology, appearing only three times in the OT. Rabbinic tradition identifies it with tefillin (phylacteries). The word may be a compound from Semitic or non-Semitic roots meaning "band" or "ornament." In parallel with אוֹת (ot, "sign") in v. 9, the totafot serve as physical reminders of YHWH's deliverance.

- **ammud anan / ammud esh** (עַמּוּד עָנָן / עַמּוּד אֵשׁ, vv. 21–22): "pillar of cloud / pillar of fire" — the visible theophanic presence that guides Yisra'el through the wilderness. The cloud conceals and reveals simultaneously; the fire illuminates darkness. Together they represent YHWH's unceasing presence — by day and by night, in light and in dark, YHWH goes before His people. The ammud is not merely a navigational aid but a manifestation of divine presence — the God who walks (holekh) with His people.

- **atsmot Yosef** (עַצְמוֹת יוֹסֵף, v. 19): "bones of Yosef" — the physical remains that connect the patriarchal era to the exodus. Yosef's dying wish (Bereshit 50:25) was that his bones not remain in Mitsrayim but be carried to the land of promise. Mosheh's act of taking the bones honors a 400-year-old oath and affirms that the exodus is the fulfillment of the Abrahamic covenant. The bones will journey through the wilderness, across the Yarden, and be buried at Shekhem (Joshua 24:32).
